headline: New AI Radar Gives Arctic Communities a Fighting Chance
meta_description: Bear-dar, an AI-powered radar system, detects polar bears approaching Arctic settlements, preventing dangerous encounters as climate change pushes bears onto l…
summary_short: An innovative radar system successfully spotted a polar bear family near an Arctic weather station, giving residents time to safely guide the animals back to sea ice.
body:
When a polar bear family approached a Canadian weather station in the Arctic town of Eureka this past May, the people working there had something their predecessors never did: advance warning.

Bear-dar, an AI-driven radar system developed by nonprofit Polar Bears International and security firm Spotter Global, detected the mother and two cubs moving toward the station. The discovery gave staff members crucial time to plan a safe response—they guided the bears back onto sea ice and monitored their movements using the system's cameras.

"They wouldn't have been able to see those bears just standing outside and looking around," said Alysa McCall, director of science at Polar Bears International. "The early-warning system gave them a lot of time to make a plan."

The technology addresses a growing crisis. As Arctic sea ice melts due to climate change, polar bears lose their natural habitat and increasingly venture onto land searching for food, bringing them into dangerous contact with humans. Bear-dar's radar panels—each about the size of an iPad—scan landscapes up to 1.2 kilometers away, alerting people when bears approach.

The AI was trained using footage from zoo-held polar bears, ensuring it could reliably recognize the animals. McCall's team now aims to make the system more affordable and accessible, hoping eventually to offer it to Arctic communities where human-bear encounters are becoming unavoidable.
